sections.title: Phrasal verbs
items: **give away** — give something free of charge
**save up (for)** — save money (for a specific purpose)
**take back** — return something to the place it came from

exercises.title: Topic vocabulary (gap fill)
exercises.instructions: Complete the sentences with words from the unit.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the sentences with words from the unit.

items.sentence: There was an ...... for a new phone on the bus stop.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: An advertisement on the bus stop.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Underline time expressions first — they usually tell you which tense to use.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: I can't ...... those trainers because they cost too much.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Can't afford = don't have enough money.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: This second-hand bike was a real ...... at only €40.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: A real bargain = very good price.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Always keep the ...... in case you need to take something back.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Keep the receipt for returns.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: I gave the assistant €10 and waited for my ...... .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Wait for your change (money back).
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Underline time expressions first — they usually tell you which tense to use.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: The market sells a wide ...... of fresh fruit and vegetables.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: A wide variety of fruit.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "variety" is the only word or phrase that fits both grammar and meaning in: "The market sells a wide ...... of fresh fruit and vegetables.".

items.sentence: I'm trying to ...... enough money for a tablet.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Save enough money over time.
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Underline time expressions first — they usually tell you which tense to use.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: The sofa is used, but it's still in excellent ...... .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: In excellent condition = good state.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

exercises.title: Prepositional phrases
exercises.instructions: Choose the correct phrase to complete each sentence.
exercises.studentInstruction: Choose the correct phrase to complete each sentence.

items.originalSentence: Do you want to pay **in cash / for rent / in debt** or by card?
items.completedSentence: Do you want to pay in cash or by card?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "in cash" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "in cash" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "for rent" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.for rent: "for rent" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("or by card?").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: Their old car is **for sale / in cash / by credit card**.
items.completedSentence: Their old car is for sale .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "for sale" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "for sale" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "in cash" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.in cash: "in cash" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence (".").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: That small office is **for rent / in debt / in cash**.
items.completedSentence: That small office is for rent .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "for rent" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "for rent" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "in debt" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.in debt: "in debt" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence (".").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: She paid for the hotel **by credit card / for sale / in debt**.
items.completedSentence: She paid for the hotel by credit card .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "by credit card" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "by credit card" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "for sale" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.for sale: "for sale" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence (".").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: He was **in debt / for rent / in good condition** after borrowing too much money.
items.completedSentence: He was in debt after borrowing too much money.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "in debt" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "in debt" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "for rent" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.for rent: "for rent" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("after borrowing too much money.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: The phone is second-hand but **in good condition / for sale / in cash**.
items.completedSentence: The phone is second-hand but in good condition .

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "in good condition" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "in good condition" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "for sale" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.for sale: "for sale" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence (".").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

exercises.title: Word patterns
exercises.instructions: Complete each sentence with one suitable preposition.

items.sentence: This jacket belongs ...... my brother.

items.sentence: Did you borrow that book ...... the library?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Borrow from the library.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the full sentence with your answer inserted — if it sounds wrong, check tense, preposition, or word form.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

exercises.title: Word formation
exercises.instructions: Complete the sentence with the correct form of the word in capitals.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form of the word in capitals.

items.sentence: The final bill included an ...... for service. (ADD)

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is noun from ADD, so the correct answer is "addition".

wrongOptions.add: The root ADD cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "addition".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of ADD that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: ADD → addition: Change the word class to a noun with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "addition" is the word-formation answer from the root ADD in this context.

items.sentence: This online course is cheap and very ...... . (AFFORD)

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is adjective from AFFORD, so the correct answer is "affordable".

wrongOptions.afford: The root AFFORD cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "affordable".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of AFFORD that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: AFFORD → affordable: Change the word class to an adjective with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "affordable" is the word-formation answer from the root AFFORD in this context.

items.sentence: There is no ...... between the two products; they are almost the same. (COMPARE)

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is noun from COMPARE, so the correct answer is "comparison".

wrongOptions.compare: The root COMPARE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "comparison".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of COMPARE that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: COMPARE → comparison: Change the word class to a noun with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "comparison" is the word-formation answer from the root COMPARE in this context.
